# Energy Transition and Grid Infrastructure

The global energy transition from fossil fuels to renewable sources presents one of the most significant infrastructure challenges in human history. The shift requires not just new generation capacity but a fundamental redesign of electricity grids, storage systems, and energy markets.

## Grid Modernization

Traditional power grids were designed for centralized generation — large coal, gas, or nuclear plants feeding electricity through high-voltage transmission lines to local distribution networks. Renewable energy sources like solar and wind are distributed, intermittent, and often located far from demand centers.

Grid modernization requires massive investment in transmission infrastructure, smart grid technology, and energy storage. The intermittency of solar and wind means that grid operators must balance supply and demand in real-time, managing the variability that comes with weather-dependent generation.

## Nuclear Energy Renaissance

Nuclear power provides carbon-free baseload electricity but faces challenges including high capital costs, long construction timelines, public opposition, and waste disposal concerns. Small modular reactors (SMRs) represent a potential path forward, offering lower upfront costs and the ability to site reactors closer to demand.

The energy demands of data centers and AI compute infrastructure have renewed interest in nuclear power. Several technology companies have announced partnerships with nuclear developers to secure reliable, carbon-free electricity for their operations.

## Supply Chain Constraints

The energy transition depends on critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, rare earth elements, copper, and nickel — that face significant supply chain constraints. Mining and processing capacity must scale dramatically to meet projected demand for batteries, solar panels, wind turbines, and electric vehicles.

Geopolitical factors complicate these supply chains. China dominates processing for many critical minerals. Diversifying supply chains requires years of investment in mining, processing, and refining capacity.
